fields.Newspaper Mentions: c. Sept 1, 1862 (event, est. — regiment departed Philadelphia c. early September 1862 en route south; exact date uncertain) | HSP Fales Collection, DOC-05, letter from Col. Thomas A. Roberts, Sept 11, 1862, Historical Society of Pennsylvania | UVRS (addressed to "President, Union Relief Association" — likely alternate name for or affiliated body of the UVRS) — Writing from Fort Carroll nine days after departure, Col. Roberts reported that about half a dozen sick men had been left in the saloon's care: "your people kindly took them in charge until they should be well enough to join their Companies." He added: "I will take this opportunity to express to you & through you to the Association which you represent the heartfelt thanks of the Officers & men of this Regt for the kind treatment which we experienced at your hands, & may the choicest of Heaven's blessings rest upon you & all others who are instrumental in lightening the burdens & hardships of a soldier's life." Confirms the saloon retained soldiers too ill to march — a documented caretaking function beyond mere refreshment. [B-115] ||| August 22, 1862 (event) | Houghton, Edwin B. The Campaigns of the Seventeenth Maine, archive.org campaignssevent00houggoog | CS ("Cooper Volunteer Refreshment Rooms" — explicit; Cooper named) — The regiment arrived in Philadelphia at half-past three p.m. and "partook of a generous collation at the Cooper Volunteer Refreshment Rooms." Ladies turned out en masse with encouragement; regiment waited until 11 p.m. for transportation in a rainstorm. CS Ledger: August 22, 1862, 1030 men, South (Outbound). [B-146]

fields.Newspaper Mentions: Sept 12, 1862 (event, 7am, five days before Antietam) | John W. Storrs, "The 'Twentieth Connecticut': A Regimental History" (1886), archive.org twentiethconnect00stor | CS — "From New York the regiment proceeded by steamer to Port Monmouth, N.J., and from thence by cars to Philadelphia; reaching that city at 7 o'clock on the morning of the 12th, where a good breakfast at the 'Old Cooper's Shop' was furnished by the Union Volunteers Refreshment committee." Names the saloon explicitly (with a small naming conflation — calling the hosting body the "Union Volunteers Refreshment committee" while identifying the location as the Cooper Shop — likely just imprecise period phrasing rather than a real ambiguity about which saloon). Companion detail: the regiment reached Baltimore at 10am the next day and was fed by the "Union Relief committee" there, with the author noting the marked contrast between this calm reception and Baltimore's 1861 mob violence, attributing the change more to the guns of Fort McHenry than to any real conversion to loyalty. [B-056]

fields.Notes: Created 2026-07-20 to resolve UVRS diary p005 (1861-09-16, "69th. Col. Owens men to dinner and tea"). Col. Joshua T. Owen commanded the 69th Pennsylvania Infantry ('Paddy Owen's Regulars', predominantly Irish, part of the Philadelphia Brigade), organized Aug 18-19 1861 and left the state Sept 17 1861 -- diary date is the day before departure, a perfect fit. Confirms a prior vault crossref note ('69th regiment, Col. Owens to Gen. Stone') -- the 69th PA was attached to Gen. Charles P. Stone's division, distinct from the 69th New York (Corcoran's Irish regiment). High confidence.

fields.Newspaper Mentions: c. April 19, 1864 (event, outbound transit to Army of the Potomac via Annapolis; left Massachusetts April 18, arrived Philadelphia in the evening, departed for Baltimore April 20) | Anderson, John. The Fifty-seventh Regiment of Massachusetts Volunteers in the War of the Rebellion: Army of the Potomac. Boston: E. B. Stillings & Co., Printers, 1896. Pp. 15–17 (approx.). archive.org: fiftyseventhregi00ander | CS ("Cooper Refreshment Rooms") — "The regiment arrived at Philadelphia in the evening and was hospitably Entertained by the citizens at Cooper Refreshment Rooms. Never will the Fifty-Seventh Massachusetts and other regiments forget the bountiful feast which was provided for the hungry men arriving in that city on their way to the seat of war. It was a noble exhibition of Philadelphia loyalty and patriotism shining down through the long years from 1776." Passage includes extended digression on Cooper Shop founding and history. CS Ledger also records a separate homeward visit (August 1, 1865; Direction: North/Homeward). [B-124]

August 30, 1861 (event, outbound transit; CS Ledger confirmed — regiment mustered August 28, departed Massachusetts by Fall River steamboat to New York, then Philadelphia, arriving morning of August 30) | Adams, John G. B. Reminiscences of the Nineteenth Massachusetts Regiment. Boston: Wright & Potter Printing Co., 1899. Ch. I–II, pp. 6–7 (approx.). archive.org: reminiscencesofn00adam | CS ("the cooper's shop") — "Upon our arrival in Philadelphia we heard a signal gun and learned that it was to inform the people connected with the cooper's shop that we were coming. We marched to that place and found a nice breakfast served by the first ladies of the city. This was the only home-like meal we had received since leaving Massachusetts, and our hearts went out to the loyal people, and our thanks were expressed in three rousing cheers for them. But we hastened on, and soon took the cars for Washington." Signal-gun alert system explicitly named. CS Ledger also records a Feb 6, 1864 outbound trip and a July 1, 1865 homeward trip (as "19th Mass. Veteran Volunteers") — book passage documents the initial 1861 transit. Also of note: later in the same book (Antietam chapter), the author mentions Mrs. Mary Lee of Philadelphia as a woman working for soldiers at Antietam and still doing so years later — relevant to B-003 (Mrs. Mary Lee as UVRS prime mover). [B-126] || August 30, 1861 (same event, second independent source; CS Ledger confirmed) | Waitt, Ernest Linden. History of the Nineteenth Regiment, Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry, 1861-1865. 1906. archive.org: 04390879.3094.emory.edu (Emory University server) | CS ("The Cooper Shop" — capitalized as proper noun) — "The regiment arrived in Philadelphia at 3.30 in the morning being quartered and fed at 'The Cooper Shop' refreshment saloon. Its coming into the city had been signalled to the people at 'The Cooper Shop' by the firing of a cannon, as was the custom when a regiment arrived, en route to the seat of war." Key detail: cannon signal described as "the custom" — establishes signal system as institutionalized procedure. 3:30 a.m. arrival time more specific than Adams's "morning" account. [B-135] || February 6, 1864 (outbound veteran-furlough transit; CS Ledger confirmed for this date; no saloon name in diary text) | Waitt 1906 (same source as B-135). Embedded diary, djvu line ~14975, p. 296 | CS (no name given; CS Ledger confirms Feb 6, 1864 entry for 19th MA) — "Feb. 6, 1864. Started for Philadelphia this morning, arriving a little before dark, got supper and rested awhile, then started for New York, riding all night." Per expanded qualification rules: unit in Airtable + CS Ledger corroboration = qualifies. [B-170] || March 16–17, 1864 (furlough-return transit; 24-hour stop; CS explicit) | Waitt 1906 (same source). Narrative passage, djvu line ~15075, p. 298 | CS ("the old Cooper Shop" — explicit) — "At Philadelphia a stop of 24 hours was made and again were the men subjects of that splendid charity of the sons and daughters of Philadelphia, — the old Cooper Shop. The single gun which always saluted the arriving regiments gathered at once from their homes the noblest of her citizens to give aid to the tired soldiers." Notable signal-gun mobilization detail; "again" confirms prior visits. [B-171] || July 1, 1865 (homeward muster-out transit; CS Ledger confirmed; CS explicit) | Waitt 1906 (same source). Narrative passage, djvu line ~18766 | CS ("the 'Old Cooper Shop'" — explicit) — "The command left Washington at 10 A.M., Baltimore at 4.45 P.M. on the same day; arrived in Philadelphia at 6 A.M. on July 1st. It is needless to say that from the Philadelphians the regiment experienced a cordial and substantial welcome at the 'Old Cooper Shop.' Leaving Philadelphia at 2 P.M. on July 1, the men reached New York on the same night..." CS Ledger confirms July 1, 1865 entry (noted as '19th Mass. Veteran Volunteers'). [B-172]

Headley, "Massachusetts in the Rebellion" (1866), archive.org massachusettsinr00head | NOT the Cooper Shop/UVRS — sheltered instead at the unoccupied Girard House hotel | CORRECTION to an earlier note in this project: 8th MA Militia was flagged as a dead end ("no dedicated regimental history turns up as freely digitized") since no standalone published history could be found; this comprehensive state history does cover the regiment, but the specific episode found describes lodging at a hotel, not either refreshment saloon. Word of the Baltimore riot reached the regiment while in Philadelphia; with the rail route through Baltimore now unsafe and telegraph communication cut, Gen. Butler's men were sheltered overnight in the Girard House and "abundantly furnished with refreshments" while Butler spent the night buying tools and supplies to rebuild burned railroad tracks and bridges, and personally offered a revolver to any officer willing to lead the advance into hostile Maryland. Logged for completeness given the correction, though it does not confirm a Cooper Shop/UVRS visit specifically — flag for future research if a separate saloon visit by this regiment turns up elsewhere. Sept 12, 1862 (event, early evening) | Leander W. Cogswell, "A History of the Eleventh New Hampshire Regiment Volunteer Infantry" (1891), archive.org 02511527.3272.emory.edu | CS — One event corroborated by THREE separate firsthand voices in the same book, similar to the pattern found for 3rd NH and 7th RI: (1) the main narrative names the saloon directly as "the Cooper saloon" and describes the regiment marching afterward through crowded streets to depart in cattle cars the next morning; (2) Lt. William C. Wood's diary/letters describe a midnight march through packed Philadelphia streets, with people of every age and station reaching out to shake hands and say goodbye, noting that some 25 regiments had reportedly passed through that single day; (3) Sgt. F.H. Goodall's brief account independently confirms the same evening arrival and kind supper. Rich companion color from the main narrative: a bit of banter with bystanders amazed at the unusually tall men in the regiment at the Stonington ferry; a cold, silent reception in Baltimore that night, marching along the same street where the 6th Massachusetts had been attacked in April 1861; and a memorably bad breakfast the next morning at Washington's "Soldiers' Retreat," later described at a regimental reunion as the worst meal any veteran endured during his whole service. Verified against archive.org full text. [B-074]

fields.Newspaper Mentions: Oct 12, 1862 (event, ~midnight, en route to Baltimore — original departure) | Stephen G. Cook (comp.), "The 'Dutchess County Regiment' (150th Regiment of New York State Volunteer Infantry) in the Civil War" (1907), archive.org dutchesscountyre00cook | CS (identity inferred, not recognized by name in the source) — The veterans' own 1907 account calls the building "the Soldiers' Retreat" and describes it as a former cooper's/barrel shop near the depot, converted into a free mess-hall decorated with American flags and portraits of Washington and Lincoln, crediting the effort to a "Union Relief Association." The description (an old cooperage, near the depot, offering free meals to passing troops) matches the Cooper Shop's known building and origin closely enough to identify it as such, even though the veterans writing decades later did not recognize or use its proper name — an interesting case of an anecdote independently confirming the physical site without recognizing the institution's actual identity. The account adds that similar mess-halls existed in most large Northern cities, so Philadelphia's version, while appreciated, wasn't understood by these particular veterans as anything unusually exceptional. Verified against archive.org full text. [B-080]

Jan 28, 1862 (event, arrived ~8pm, stayed overnight) | Lieut. George Breck, wartime newspaper columns for the Rochester Union & Advertiser, compiled as "Battery L, 1st [New York] Artillery Regiment (Light) — George Breck Columns," Chapter 5 ("Mud, Mud, Mud"), New York State Military Museum digital collection | UVRS — "Leaving Albany Tuesday morning (Jan. 28), via Hudson River RR., we arrived in New York that noon, and that afternoon we took the cars for Philadelphia. We reached there about 8 p.m., proceeded to the Union Volunteer Refreshment Saloon, where we were supplied with an excellent supper, remained at the same place all night, were again generously treated with coffee and refreshments in the morning, and at 8 o'clock started for Baltimore, arriving there at noon, where we were invited to partake of a dinner at the Soldiers' Retreat located in that city." Names the saloon explicitly, with the unusual detail of an overnight stay (not just a meal) — the battery arrived too late in the evening to continue immediately and was housed at the UVRS itself until morning. Also names Baltimore's counterpart institution ("Soldiers' Retreat"), explicitly noted as government-funded rather than citizen-supported, a useful point of contrast with Philadelphia's privately/voluntarily funded saloons. Battery L ("Rochester Union Greys," also known as Reynolds' Battery) later fought at Cedar Mountain, Second Bull Run, South Mountain, Antietam, Fredericksburg, Chancellorsville, and Gettysburg (where it has a monument). Verified verbatim against the museum's digitized transcription. [B-065]

Hanson (regimental chaplain), "Historical Sketch of the Old Sixth Regiment of Massachusetts Volunteers, During its Three Campaigns in 1861, 1862, 1863, and 1864" (1866), archive.org historicalsketch00hans | CS — Officers were entertained separately at the Continental Hotel while the enlisted men were fed at what the text calls "the Cooper Saloon," described as lavishly supplied with both staple food and delicacies. A formal printed welcome address was placed at every man's plate, composed specifically for the occasion and explicitly invoking the regiment's famous role in the April 19, 1861 Baltimore riot as the reason for the city's special gratitude — the piece is structured almost like a ceremonial toast/ode rather than a plain announcement, so it is not reproduced verbatim here, only summarized. The Philadelphia "North American" newspaper reportedly devoted two full columns to the reception, comparing the 6th Massachusetts to the 69th New York and the 2nd PA Reserves as regiments that had earned a permanent place in the war's history. [B-075]

Aug 21, 1864 (event — the regiment's third and final activation, homeward-bound after garrison duty at Arlington Heights) | Same source | CS — The author explicitly states the regiment had by this point been entertained at the Cooper Saloon five separate times across the war, each occasion feeling warmer than the last, with fruit in particular so abundant that soldiers struggled to find anywhere left to spend their money or any remaining space for the gifts pressed on them. Two days later the regiment reached Fort Delaware to finish its hundred-day term. This passage is the unit's own explicit tally of its saloon visits, confirming the pattern seen in the project's ledger records. Verified against archive.org full text. [B-076]

VERIFIED DIRECTLY (2026-07 session) against the 13th NH's own primary source, superseding the earlier secondary-citation note below.

Oct 7, 1862 (event, 6pm arrival — original departure, via Nashua, Worcester, and steamer City of Boston to Jersey City) | S. Millett Thompson, "Thirteenth Regiment of New Hampshire Volunteer Infantry in the War of the Rebellion, 1861-1865" (1888), archive.org thirteenthregime88thom | CS (named explicitly by address: "the Cooper Shop Volunteer Refreshment Saloon, No. 1009 Otsego Street") — The regiment traveled the last leg from Jersey City to Philadelphia in dirty, crowded cattle cars; one man was badly injured falling from a car en route. Immediately following the supper account, the author includes a Dec 12, 1884 letter from Wm. R.S. Cooper, son of founder Wm. M. Cooper, describing the saloon's founding in April 1861 by his father, himself, and a few friends; initially self-funded, later by citizen contributions, but never a cent from any government; capable of feeding half a regiment at once and a full regiment roughly every hour; over 600,000 men fed during the war at 10-15 cents per meal; and the family's own business ruined as a direct result of the enterprise. This is a first-hand institutional account from the founder's own son, directly relevant to ADR-001's founder-name question (confirms "Wm. M. Cooper" as the father/founder). After the meal the regiment marched roughly 1.5 miles through the city to entrain for Baltimore, crossing the Susquehanna by ferry at Havre de Grace due to the destroyed rail bridge, and reached Baltimore the next morning marching with some loaded muskets through the same streets where the 6th Massachusetts had been mobbed. Verified against archive.org full text. [B-025]

fields.Newspaper Mentions: Dec 28, 1861 (event, evening) | John K. Burlingame (comp.), "History of the Fifth Regiment of Rhode Island Heavy Artillery, during Three Years and a Half of Service in North Carolina" (1892), ECU Digital Collections | CS — "Very soon after, the train was announced, and in a few hours more they were enjoying the welcome hospitalities of the 'Cooper Shop,' in Philadelphia, a place well and most favorably known to all soldiers passing through that city,—the fame of which is a lasting monument to the large hearted and practical patriotism of its citizens, which really earned for it the title of the 'City of Brotherly Love.' Resuming their route at midnight they soon reached the Maryland line." Names the saloon explicitly. Preceded by a memorable vignette: at Jersey City that same day, Major Wright refused to move his men into uncleaned cattle cars, telling railroad officials "we will stay here, at your expense, until you find something better" — the officers were so pleased they bought him a field glass on the spot. Companion detail: reaching Baltimore at noon the next day (Dec 29), the battalion was served by citizens attempting to replicate Philadelphia's saloon ("You are our first guests") but were then unexpectedly asked to sign a receipt for the food — revealing it was a paid ration, not free hospitality as first assumed, a small but telling contrast with the Cooper Shop's genuine charity. Verified verbatim directly against the ECU Digital Collections text. [B-049]

June 17, 1865 (event — homeward journey after muster-out, following the pursuit of Lee's army and duty near Richmond) | P.C. fields.Notes: Created 2026-07-20 to resolve UVRS diary p011 (1861-11-16, "3d Artillery U.S. Regulars from California, Col. Merchant"). This exact commander+unit pairing was already confirmed in prior vault research (research/uvrs_ledger_crossref_4628-4655_2026-07-17.md, Part II): 'Col. [Muchard?]' (rough diary) resolved to 'Col. Merchant' -- Col. Charles G. Merchant commanded the 3d US Artillery. This is part of the documented California Regulars transit sequence (Nov 1861-Jan 1862). No Units record existed for this regiment prior to today.

Anecdote search dead end (2026-07-16): checked Ernest Mettendorf's modern regimental history 'Between Triumph and Disaster' (dmna.ny.gov PDF, comprehensive, covers Sept 1861 muster through Antietam in detail) — no Cooper Shop/UVRS mention anywhere. The regiment's actual outbound route bypassed Philadelphia entirely: Camp Hempstead (Long Island) → embarked at Bay Park → ferried to Port Elizabeth, NJ → direct to Annapolis, never touching the standard NY→NJ→Philadelphia→Baltimore rail corridor other regiments used. Do not re-search this unit's Sept 1861 transit for a saloon anecdote — the geography rules it out.

Oct 15, 1863 (event, 7am — regiment returning to the front after NYC draft-riot duty) | James L. Bowen, "History of the Thirty-Seventh Regiment, Mass. Volunteers" (archive.org historyofthirtys00bowe) | UVRS — "Philadelphia was reached at 7 o'clock next morning, and, in accordance with the unvarying custom of that City of Brotherly Love, a sumptuous repast was furnished at the Volunteer Refreshment Saloon. Supper was provided there,—not of the Philadelphia standard, but good and satisfactory." Names the saloon explicitly and directly compares it favorably to Baltimore's fare. Companion passage, same source: on Aug 1, 1863, the regiment passed through Philadelphia earlier (traveling the other direction, to help suppress the NYC draft riots) and received "a bounteous supper... well seasoned with those kind words of cheer" — saloon not named in that passage, but the text explicitly contrasts Philadelphia's warm 1863 welcome with the "cold look of suppressed hatred" the regiment met in Baltimore during its original 1862 departure. fields.Newspaper Mentions: September 28, 1862 (event, Sunday afternoon, 3-4pm — INITIAL OUTBOUND TRANSIT; prior session incorrectly dated this "~July/Aug 1863" by misidentifying the Lee invasion scare as Gettysburg 1863, but the chapter heading explicitly gives departure date as "27th of September, A.D., 1862" [Chapter II, 'FROM CONCORD TO FALMOUTH']. The Lee threat was the September 1862 Maryland campaign, Antietam fought Sept 17, 1862 — regiment arrived in Philadelphia 11 days later. Both archive.org identifiers confirm only ONE Cooper Shop passage in the entire book.) | Capt. A.W. Bartlett, "History of the Twelfth Regiment, New Hampshire Volunteers in the War of the Rebellion" (1897), archive.org historyoftwelfth00bart / warebellionofthe00bartrich / historytwelfthr00bartgoog | CS — Names the saloon explicitly as "Cooper's Volunteers' Refreshment Saloon." One of the richest passages found in this project's research: the men are described finding themselves amid what the author calls "fountains of water and mountains of food," with well-dressed Philadelphia ladies pressing forward to shake soldiers' hands and offer encouragement, and boys insisting on carrying knapsacks. The author explicitly ties the unusual warmth of the welcome to the just-passed invasion scare — Philadelphia had recently feared attack itself (Lee's Maryland campaign, September 1862), so the arriving troops were treated as literal saviors rather than just passing strangers. "Church services had just closed" when the regiment was ready to depart, confirming Sunday afternoon timing. The march from the saloon to the next depot stretched over a mile, lined the whole way with well-wishers; departure was marked by mutual cheering between the regiment and a crowd five times its size, with shouted exchanges of "God bless you" back and forth. Verbatim: "by the time the regiment was ready to bid a reluctant farewell to 'Cooper's Volunteers' Refreshment Saloon,' and its savory dishes, the surrounding square and streets were filled with men, women, and children." Verified against archive.org full text. [B-072] [DATE CORRECTED 2026-07-20: was incorrectly listed as ~July/Aug 1863]

Hopkins, "The Seventh Regiment Rhode Island Volunteers in the Civil War, 1862-1865" (1903), archive.org cu31924030915742 | Both — One of the richest institutional descriptions found in this project's research to date. Full quote (condensed): "The Volunteer Refreshment Saloons were two in number and named the Union and the Cooper Shop respectively. They were located so that the soldiers could be entertained with the least possible loss of time; the former at the corner of Prince and Front Streets... the latter on Oswego Street about fifty yards from Washington Avenue. They were supported by subscriptions from citizens and governed by a joint committee which prevented all friction. Fifteen thousand soldiers were entertained in a single day. Of the Seventh, 488 men and officers were fed at the Union and 487 at the Cooper." Goes on to describe wash basins, soap, towels, five long tables per hall, an annex for sick/wounded soldiers with free writing materials and postage stamps, and the origin story: "In Southwark a number of patriotic ladies erected a little street corner saloon whence they issued hot coffee... From this humble beginning sprang the magnificent charities just described." Also describes the march from the Camden ferry to the saloons as "more like a festival than a march," with soldiers singing "We're coming, Father Abraham" and "John Brown's Body." Verified verbatim directly against archive.org full text.

Sept 12, 1862 (event, ~3pm) | Alvin Millen Crane Brown, "The Story of the Twenty-First Regiment, Connecticut Volunteer Infantry, During the Civil War, 1861-1865" (1900), archive.org storytwentyfirs00crangoog | CS — THREE independent, mutually corroborating passages in the same source:

(1) Main narrative: "By ten o'clock we left, and reached Philadelphia at three o'clock in the afternoon. Here the Soldiers' Relief Association provided a good dinner which received many commendations. With pleasant memories of the City of Brotherly Love, we left about nine o'clock in the evening for Baltimore."

(2) A field officer's letter (written days later from Camp Kearney, Sept 15, 1862): "In Philadelphia the ladies were very attentive, giving us all we wanted to eat and drink, and as we marched through the streets to the depot, crowds of women and children pressed around, asking where we belonged, bidding good-by, Godspeed..."

(3) A veteran's postwar reunion reminiscence ("Incidents and Coincidents" chapter), which is what confirms the specific saloon by name: "our very enthusiastic reception and dinner at the celebrated Cooper refreshment rooms in the city of Philadelphia, where the wealthy Cooper family fed and entertained, largely at their own expense, the troops passing through the city... Some of you will remember that this patriotic and loyal family later lost all their property, and the daughters...had to go out to work... this Regimental Association took up a collection for them amounting to twenty-six dollars...and forwarded it to them." A second veteran's reminiscence in the same chapter adds: "not one of us...will ever forget the meal we were treated to in the famous Cooper Shop restaurant. A regiment marched out as we entered. There was ample room for all... Those that were sick were taken to the hospital upstairs. Hundreds of thousands of soldiers were fed at this place during the war."

Regiment mustered Aug 23, 1862 at Norwich; this was the original departure for the front. Verified verbatim directly against archive.org full text. Notable as a genuine primary-source corroboration (from Cooper Shop veterans decades later) of the Cooper family's postwar financial ruin, matching what's already established elsewhere in this project's research (House Divided essay).
